response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), effective May 2018, reshaped how organizations handle personal data by applying to any company processing EU residents’ data regardless of its location. It guarantees rights such as access, erasure (“right to be forgotten”), and data portability, and requires explicit consent for processing; organizations processing data at scale must appoint Data Protection Officers and report breaches within 72 hours. Penalties can be up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million (whichever is higher), with major fines including Meta’s €1.2 billion (2023) and Amazon’s €746 million, while Google faced multiple fines totaling over €200 million. While GDPR has influenced privacy laws worldwide (e.g., California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s act) and established data privacy as a fundamental right, critics say it burdens small businesses, drives cookie-consent frustration, and faces inconsistent enforcement.
